lab_analysis.summary: The complete blood count reveals a low Red Blood Cell (RBC) count, which may indicate an initial or mild form of anemia, although hemoglobin and hematocrit are within normal limits. A notable finding is the MCV (Mean Corpuscular Volume) at 17 fl, which is critically low and suggests microcytic red blood cells. This is contradicted by a high MCH (Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin) at 41.4 pg. This unusual combination of low MCV and high MCH is atypical and might indicate an error in the lab report or a complex hematological issue requiring further investigation. All other blood parameters, including White Blood Cell count, platelet count, and differential counts, appear to be within normal ranges. The patient also has a condition of High Blood Pressure.
concerns: Low Red Blood Cell (RBC) count, indicating potential anemia.
The inconsistent lab values for MCV (critically low) and MCH (high) require re-evaluation or further diagnostic clarification.
Management of High Blood Pressure (BP) through dietary and lifestyle interventions.

monday.breakfast: Idli (steamed, less salt) with Sambar (low sodium, vegetable-rich).
monday.lunch: Brown rice, Aviyal (mixed vegetable curry with coconut, no added salt), Fish curry (sardine/mackerel, less oil/salt).
monday.dinner: Wheat Roti with Green Gram Dal curry (Parippu curry), stir-fried leafy greens (e.g., spinach/amaranth).

tuesday.breakfast: Appam with Vegetable Stew (coconut milk based, less salt).
tuesday.lunch: Kerala Matta rice, Thoran (cabbage/bean stir-fry), Chicken curry (skinless, less oil/salt).
tuesday.dinner: Vegetable Dosa (less oil) with Tomato Chutney (homemade, low sodium).

wednesday.breakfast: Puttu (steamed rice cake) with Kadala curry (black chickpea curry, less salt).
wednesday.lunch: Brown rice, Sambar, stir-fried bitter gourd/snake gourd (Pavaikka/Padavalanga mezhukkupuratti).
wednesday.dinner: Ragi Dosa with Coconut Chutney (less salt, fresh coconut).

thursday.breakfast: Oats Porridge with fruits and unsalted nuts.
thursday.lunch: Kerala Matta rice, Moru Curry (yogurt-based curry), steamed fish (e.g., Mackerel).
thursday.dinner: Whole wheat bread (2 slices) with avocado spread and boiled eggs.
thursday.snacks: Orange, a small bowl of sprouts.

friday.breakfast: Idiyappam (string hoppers) with Vegetable Kurma (less salt).
friday.dinner: Mixed vegetable Upma (semolina) with a side of plain yogurt.

saturday.lunch: Kerala Matta rice, Cabbage Thoran, Fish Fry (shallow fried, minimal oil, less salt, turmeric, pepper).
saturday.dinner: Chicken stir-fry with plenty of vegetables (broccoli, bell peppers, carrots), minimal low-sodium soy sauce.

sunday.lunch: Brown rice, Sambar, Chicken stew (coconut milk based, less salt).
sunday.dinner: Vegetable soup (clear broth, low sodium) with a small portion of whole wheat pasta.

foods_to_include: Whole grains (brown rice, matta rice, oats, ragi, whole wheat)
Fresh fruits (banana, papaya, guava, orange, watermelon, pomegranate)
Vegetables (leafy greens, bitter gourd, snake gourd, cabbage, beans, broccoli, bell peppers, carrots)
Lean proteins (sardines, mackerel, chicken, lentils, chickpeas, eggs)
Healthy fats (coconut oil in moderation, avocado, unsalted nuts, seeds)
Iron-rich foods (leafy greens, lentils, fortified cereals, lean meats/fish)
Vitamin C-rich foods (citrus fruits, bell peppers, guava) for iron absorption
foods_to_avoid: High-sodium foods (processed foods, pickles, papad, processed meats, excessive table salt)
key_nutrients: Potassium (for BP management, found in bananas, leafy greens, sweet potatoes)
Magnesium (for BP management, found in nuts, seeds, whole grains)
Calcium (for BP management, found in low-fat dairy, leafy greens)
Omega-3 fatty acids (for heart health, found in fatty fish)
Folic Acid (for red blood cell production)
Vitamin B12 (for red blood cell production)
recommendations.hydration: Drink at least 2.5-3 liters of water daily. Tender coconut water is also beneficial for electrolytes. Limit sugary beverages.
recommendations.exercise: Engage in regular physical activity for at least 30-45 minutes most days of the week, such as brisk walking, swimming, or cycling, to help manage blood pressure and improve overall health. Consult a physician before starting any new exercise regimen.
